meanings.definition: A person or thing that comes before and signals the approach of another.
examples: The telegraph was a precursor to the modern telephone.
A sore throat is often a precursor to a bad cold.

meanings.definition: To claim that something is true, especially without proof and often in a legal setting.
examples: The prosecutors allege that he stole company funds.
She alleged that her neighbor had damaged her fence.

meanings.definition: Extremely cold; lacking warmth.
examples: The frigid winter wind cut through their coats.
A frigid blast of air met them as they opened the freezer.

meanings.definition: Cold or unfriendly in manner.
examples: She gave him a frigid stare and walked away.

meanings.definition: Speech or behavior that incites rebellion against the authority of a state.
examples: The pamphlet was banned for spreading sedition against the government.
He was arrested on charges of sedition for urging soldiers to revolt.

meanings.definition: To add or attach something as a supplement at the end.
examples: She appended a footnote to clarify the source.
Please append your signature to the bottom of the form.

meanings.definition: A short, well-known saying that expresses a general truth or piece of advice.
examples: "Look before you leap" is a familiar proverb.
Her grandmother loved to quote old proverbs about hard work.

meanings.definition: A loud, harsh sound, like the cry of a donkey or the blast of a horn.
examples: The donkey's bray echoed across the valley.
A sudden bray of the trumpet startled the crowd.

meanings.definition: A narrow channel of water connecting two larger bodies of water.
examples: Ships pass through the strait to reach the open sea.
The strait between the two islands is dangerous to navigate.

meanings.definition: Showing no pity or sympathy toward others.
examples: The hard-hearted landlord evicted the family without warning.
It would be hard-hearted to ignore their plea for help.

meanings.definition: To make something larger or greater by adding to it.
examples: He took a second job to augment his income.
The new wing will augment the museum's exhibition space.

meanings.definition: To lower someone in rank, dignity, or self-respect; to degrade.
examples: He refused to abase himself by begging for the favor.
The cruel ruler sought to abase his rivals before the court.

meanings.definition: A distinctive and habitual gesture or peculiarity in a person's behavior or speech.
examples: Tapping his pen was a nervous mannerism he could not break.
The actor captured the politician's every mannerism.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that signals the approach of another; a herald.
examples: The robin is often seen as a harbinger of spring.
Falling stock prices can be a harbinger of recession.

meanings.definition: To make something last longer than necessary; to prolong.
examples: The lawyers tried to protract the trial for weeks.
Don't protract the meeting with unnecessary debate.

meanings.definition: To stick firmly to a surface or to one another.
examples: The label won't adhere to the wet bottle.
Make sure the tape adheres tightly to the box.

meanings.definition: To follow or comply with a rule, belief, or plan.
examples: All staff must adhere to the safety guidelines.

meanings.definition: To summarize and restate the main points of something.
examples: Let me recapitulate the key arguments before we vote.
The professor recapitulated the lecture in the final minutes.

meanings.definition: Relating to, or directed toward, the side.
examples: The plant sends out lateral shoots from its main stem.
He made a lateral move to a similar role in another department.

meanings.definition: A distinctive quality or atmosphere that seems to surround a person or thing.
examples: The old castle had an aura of mystery.
She carried an aura of calm confidence into the room.

meanings.definition: Belonging to the earliest ages of the world; ancient and primitive.
examples: The explorers trekked through a primeval forest untouched by humans.
A primeval instinct told her to run from the danger.

meanings.definition: A flat map of the stars showing the heavens as projected onto a circular chart.
examples: She used a planisphere to identify the constellations overhead.
The planisphere can be rotated to match the night sky for any date.

meanings.definition: A group or set of five; the number five.
examples: The ancient text divided history into a series of pentads.
Each pentad in the cycle represented five years.

meanings.definition: A proper sense of one's own worth and dignity.
examples: She refused the insulting offer out of self-respect.
Standing up for his beliefs restored his self-respect.

meanings.definition: The act or amount of contracting into a smaller size or quantity.
examples: Wool sweaters are prone to shrinkage when washed in hot water.
The store reported significant shrinkage due to theft.

meanings.definition: A particular way of viewing things based on one's point of view or sense of relative importance.
examples: Travel gave her a fresh perspective on her own life.
From a historian's perspective, the event was a turning point.

meanings.definition: The technique of representing depth and distance on a flat surface.
examples: The artist used perspective to make the road seem to recede into the distance.

meanings.definition: The quality of being honest and having strong moral principles.
examples: A leader of great integrity, she never broke a promise.
He showed his integrity by returning the extra change.

meanings.definition: The state of being whole and undamaged.
examples: Engineers tested the structural integrity of the bridge.

meanings.definition: The hinder or rear part of something; a person's buttocks.
examples: He slipped on the ice and landed on his posterior.
The label is printed on the posterior of the device.

meanings.definition: To begin or set in motion something for the first time.
examples: The company will initiate a new training program next month.
She initiated the conversation by introducing herself.

meanings.definition: To formally admit someone into a group, often with a ceremony.
examples: The club initiated three new members last night.

meanings.definition: Relating to education, scholarship, or institutions of learning.
examples: Her academic record earned her a scholarship.
The college is known for its high academic standards.

meanings.definition: Of theoretical interest only, with no practical relevance.
examples: Whether we can afford the trip is academic now that it's cancelled.

meanings.definition: Cheerful and full of energy and enthusiasm.
examples: Her ebullient personality lit up the whole party.
The team was ebullient after their unexpected victory.

meanings.definition: Done by means of force; involving physical force.
examples: The police reported a forcible entry through the back door.
His forcible removal from the building caused a scene.

meanings.definition: To swing or move back and forth steadily between two points.
examples: The pendulum oscillates from side to side.
The fan oscillates to spread cool air across the room.

meanings.definition: To waver between differing opinions or feelings.
examples: She oscillated between excitement and fear about the move.

meanings.definition: Completely different in nature, direction, or action; contrary.
examples: The two cars sped off in opposite directions.
His opinion was the exact opposite of mine.

meanings.definition: To bring a people or place under control by conquering them.
examples: The empire sought to subjugate the neighboring tribes.
They refused to be subjugated by a foreign power.

meanings.definition: A person who gathers and keeps objects of a particular kind.
examples: As an avid stamp collector, he owned thousands of rare specimens.
The art collector donated her paintings to the museum.

meanings.definition: A short, sharp-pointed nail with a broad flat head.
examples: She pinned the poster to the wall with a tack.
He stepped on a tack and yelped in pain.

meanings.definition: A course of action or approach.
examples: When persuasion failed, she tried a different tack.

meanings.definition: Electricity produced by a difference in temperature between two materials.
examples: The device converts waste heat into thermoelectricity.
Researchers are studying thermoelectricity as a clean power source.

meanings.definition: Cautious and careful to consider all circumstances before acting.
examples: She was circumspect about sharing details with strangers.
A circumspect investor studies the risks before buying.

meanings.definition: Not welcoming or generous toward guests or strangers.
examples: The innkeeper was cold and inhospitable to the weary travelers.
They found the locals surprisingly inhospitable.

meanings.definition: Harsh and difficult to live in.
examples: The expedition crossed an inhospitable stretch of frozen tundra.

meanings.definition: To decline or deteriorate into a worse or lower condition.
examples: The friendly debate degenerated into a shouting match.
Without care, the garden quickly degenerated into a tangle of weeds.

meanings.definition: Able to live both on land and in water.
examples: Frogs are amphibious creatures that begin life in water.
The amphibious salamander hunts on the riverbank and below the surface.

meanings.definition: Designed to operate on both land and water.
examples: The troops landed in amphibious vehicles.

meanings.definition: Except for; apart from.
examples: Barring any delays, we should arrive by noon.
Everyone attended, barring two who were ill.

meanings.definition: Government or rule by women; female supremacy.
examples: The novel imagines a future gynecocracy where women hold all power.
Historians debated whether the ancient society was a true gynecocracy.

meanings.definition: Having great power, strength, or influence.
examples: The puissant general led his armies to victory.
She delivered a puissant speech that moved the nation.

meanings.definition: A belief or judgment held with confidence but not amounting to certain knowledge.
examples: In my opinion, the first plan was better.
Experts differ in their opinion on the cause.

meanings.definition: The science and technology of extracting metals from ores and working with them.
examples: Advances in metallurgy made stronger steel possible.
He studied metallurgy to design better alloys.

meanings.definition: A feeling that something, especially something bad, is about to happen; a foreboding.
examples: She had a presentiment that the journey would end badly.
A vague presentiment of danger made him turn back.

meanings.definition: Wide and deep enough for boats or ships to travel through.
examples: The river is navigable as far as the old mill.
Heavy ice made the channel no longer navigable.

meanings.definition: A chest of drawers used for storing clothing.
examples: She folded the sweaters into the top drawer of the bureau.
An antique bureau stood against the bedroom wall.

meanings.definition: An office or department for a particular kind of business.
examples: He filed a complaint with the consumer protection bureau.

meanings.definition: To give a definite and clear form to thoughts, plans, or ideas.
examples: The discussion helped crystallize her vision for the project.
His vague worries finally crystallized into a single fear.

meanings.definition: To form crystals as a solid takes shape from a solution.
examples: The sugar syrup began to crystallize as it cooled.

meanings.definition: A person who drives or tends mules.
examples: The muleteer guided his loaded animals up the mountain trail.
An experienced muleteer knows how to calm a stubborn mule.

meanings.definition: To stop resisting and give in to something overpowering.
examples: He finally succumbed to the temptation of dessert.
After a long illness, she succumbed to the disease.

meanings.definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
examples: Gossip can have a pernicious effect on a community.
Smoking is a pernicious habit that damages the lungs over time.

meanings.definition: Showing no emotion or feeling.
examples: His impassive face gave away nothing during the trial.
She remained impassive even as the bad news was read aloud.

meanings.definition: A close, warm, and confidential friendship or relationship.
examples: The two sisters shared an intimacy that no one could break.
Years of letters created an intimacy between the pen pals.

meanings.definition: To prove or show something clearly through evidence or example.
examples: The experiment demonstrates how plants respond to light.
She demonstrated her skill by solving the puzzle in seconds.

meanings.definition: To take part in a public protest.
examples: Thousands gathered to demonstrate against the new law.

meanings.definition: Having a resemblance or repetition of vowel sounds.
examples: The poet chose assonant words like "lake" and "fate" for a soft echo.
The phrase is pleasingly assonant when read aloud.

meanings.definition: A man who is the respected head of a family, tribe, or community.
examples: The old patriarch gathered his grandchildren around the fire.
As patriarch of the clan, he settled every dispute.

meanings.definition: A person skilled in several languages or who studies language.
examples: A gifted linguist, she spoke six languages fluently.
The linguist analyzed how the dialect had changed over centuries.

meanings.definition: Relating to or living in trees.
examples: Monkeys are arboreal animals that rarely come to the ground.
The arboreal canopy sheltered countless birds.

meanings.definition: Having a kind, generous, and sympathetic nature.
examples: The kind-hearted nurse stayed late to comfort the patient.
He was too kind-hearted to turn away anyone in need.

meanings.definition: The practice of depicting people and scenes as they really are, without idealization.
examples: The novel's gritty realism made the slums feel painfully vivid.
Critics praised the painting for its unflinching realism.

meanings.definition: A practical, clear-eyed acceptance of situations as they truly are.
examples: Her hard-headed realism kept the team from chasing impossible goals.

meanings.definition: Relating to the legal process of proving and validating a will.
examples: The estate was tied up in probate court for nearly a year.
She consulted a lawyer who specialized in probate matters.

meanings.definition: Relating to the ear or to the sense of hearing.
examples: The doctor noted an auricular infection causing the child's pain.
An auricular confession is whispered privately into the priest's ear.

meanings.definition: Entitled to feudal allegiance and service; sovereign.
examples: The knights swore to serve their liege lord until death.
She bowed before her liege king in the great hall.

meanings.definition: Showing a great deal of variety; capable of taking various forms.
examples: The festival featured a diverse range of music and food.
Their interests were so diverse that they rarely agreed on anything.

meanings.definition: The act of directing and managing an operation or group of people.
examples: The bridge was built under the superintendence of a veteran engineer.
She left the daily superintendence of the shop to her assistant.

meanings.definition: A person who collects, studies, or deals in old objects such as coins, books, or relics.
examples: The antiquary spent decades cataloguing medieval manuscripts.
An eager antiquary, he haggled over every rusty Roman coin.

meanings.definition: Having abandoned one's former faith, principles, or allegiance; faithless.
examples: The apostate priest was condemned by the church he had renounced.
His former allies branded him an apostate traitor to the cause.

meanings.definition: The division of words into syllables.
examples: The dictionary shows the syllabication of each entry with dots.
Teaching syllabication helps young readers sound out long words.

meanings.definition: The ceremony of crowning and formally installing a monarch.
examples: Crowds lined the streets for the queen's coronation.
The coronation lasted hours and ended with a lavish banquet.

meanings.definition: To gather together into a crowd or group.
examples: Students congregate in the courtyard between classes.
Thousands congregated in the square to hear the speech.

meanings.definition: A little at a time; gradually and in stages rather than all at once.
examples: The reforms were introduced piecemeal over several years.
He paid off the debt piecemeal, a few dollars each month.

meanings.definition: Done or happening gradually in separate, unconnected stages.
examples: Their piecemeal approach left the project disjointed and incomplete.

meanings.definition: A person who comes to live permanently in a foreign country.
examples: Her grandparents arrived as immigrants seeking a better life.
The city welcomed thousands of immigrants each year.

meanings.definition: The complete range or scope of something.
examples: Her face ran the gamut of emotions during the film.
The store sells everything across the gamut from tools to toys.

meanings.definition: Incapable of being mixed together, as oil and water.
examples: Oil and water are immiscible and separate into two layers.
The two immiscible liquids refused to blend no matter how hard she stirred.

meanings.definition: An excessive and often dangerous amount of a drug or medicine.
examples: He was rushed to hospital after an accidental overdose.
Even an overdose of vitamins can harm the body.

meanings.definition: To take a dangerously excessive amount of a drug.
examples: The patient nearly overdosed on the sleeping pills.

meanings.definition: A balloon or other aircraft kept aloft by being lighter than the surrounding air.
examples: The pioneering aerostat drifted silently above the rooftops.
Early aviators studied how to steer an aerostat against the wind.

meanings.definition: Having a thick, sticky, glue-like quality.
examples: The glutinous rice clumped together in soft, sticky balls.
A glutinous paste oozed from the crushed seeds.

meanings.definition: A small remaining trace of something that is no longer present.
examples: Not a vestige of the ancient wall remained.
A faint vestige of hope lingered in her voice.

meanings.definition: A small room serving as an entrance or waiting area before a larger, more important room.
examples: Visitors waited in the antechamber until the king summoned them.
The antechamber was lined with portraits of past rulers.

meanings.definition: The act of avoiding or escaping something cleverly; evasion.
examples: His skillful elusion of every question frustrated the reporters.
The fox's elusion of the hounds amazed the hunters.

meanings.definition: Lacking clearness; obscured as if by mist.
examples: The valley looked soft and misty in the early morning.
Her memory of that day was misty and indistinct.

meanings.definition: The quality of being impressive, magnificent, or admirably great.
examples: The cathedral's grandeur left the tourists speechless.
She spoke of the mountains with a sense of awe at their grandeur.

meanings.definition: A person who makes or enacts laws; a lawmaker.
examples: The legislator introduced a bill to protect local farmland.
Each legislator voted according to the wishes of their district.

meanings.definition: Long duration of life or of existence.
examples: Her remarkable longevity was credited to a simple, active life.
The longevity of the brand surprised its competitors.

meanings.definition: Giving off a strong or distinctive smell, especially a pleasant one.
examples: The garden was filled with odoriferous roses and lilies.
An odoriferous cheese filled the kitchen with its pungent aroma.

meanings.definition: A ship's officer in charge of the rigging, anchors, sails, and other equipment.
examples: The boatswain blew his whistle to summon the crew on deck.
She trusted the boatswain to keep every rope and sail in order.

meanings.definition: Strong moral integrity and uprightness; proven honesty.
examples: The judge was known for her unquestioned probity.
His financial probity earned the trust of every investor.

meanings.definition: Willing to cooperate, agree, or accept guidance.
examples: The students were amenable to trying the new schedule.
He proved amenable to compromise once he heard the offer.

meanings.definition: An elaborate, often improvised solo passage in a piece of music, usually near the end of a movement.
examples: The pianist dazzled the audience with a brilliant cadenza.
She improvised a soaring cadenza before the orchestra returned.

meanings.definition: A scholar who studies the history, structure, and development of language.
examples: The philologist traced the word back to its ancient Greek root.
As a philologist, she could read a dozen dead languages.

meanings.definition: The active promotion of human welfare, typically through generous donations.
examples: His philanthropy funded schools across the region.
She devoted her later years to philanthropy and charity work.

meanings.definition: Lacking originality or freshness because of overuse; dull from repetition.
examples: The speech was full of trite phrases and worn-out clichés.
His apology felt trite and insincere.

meanings.definition: Made up of many interconnected parts; complicated and hard to understand.
examples: The instructions were too complex for a beginner to follow.
Climate change is a complex problem with no simple solution.

meanings.definition: In exactly the same words; word for word.
examples: She repeated the speech verbatim from memory.
The reporter quoted him verbatim in the article.

meanings.definition: Corresponding word for word to the original.
examples: He produced a verbatim transcript of the interview.

meanings.definition: A person who draws up and enacts laws; a legislator.
examples: Moses is remembered as the great lawgiver of his people.
The ancient lawgiver shaped the rules of the new republic.

meanings.definition: An official who represents their country in dealings with other nations.
examples: The diplomat negotiated a peace treaty between the rivals.
As a skilled diplomat, she smoothed over the tense talks.

meanings.definition: To move or roll with a winding or twisting motion.
examples: The vines convolve around the trellis as they grow.
Smoke convolved upward in lazy, twisting coils.

meanings.definition: Clearly visible or easily understood; obvious.
examples: It soon became apparent that the plan would fail.
Her relief was apparent the moment she saw him.

meanings.definition: Seeming real or true, but not necessarily so.
examples: Despite his apparent calm, he was deeply worried.

meanings.definition: A lover, especially one in an illicit or secret relationship.
examples: The queen was rumored to have a secret paramour at court.
He met his paramour in hidden corners of the city.

meanings.definition: To gather or build up gradually in quantity or number.
examples: Dust began to accumulate on the unused shelves.
Over the years she accumulated a vast collection of books.

meanings.definition: Thoroughly and completely; genuinely so in every respect.
examples: He was out-and-out the best player on the team.
The story turned out to be out-and-out false.

meanings.definition: Complete and absolute; thoroughgoing.
examples: It was an out-and-out lie from start to finish.

meanings.definition: A state of mental agitation, anxiety, or confusion.
examples: The strange noise filled her with sudden perturbation.
His perturbation showed in his trembling hands.

meanings.definition: A small disturbance that alters the normal motion or state of a system.
examples: A slight gravitational perturbation shifted the comet's orbit.

meanings.definition: A feeling of guilt or remorse for having done something wrong.
examples: He lied without the slightest compunction.
She felt a pang of compunction after snapping at her friend.

meanings.definition: The part of a shore lying between the high-tide and low-tide marks.
examples: Children searched the foreshore for shells at low tide.
Seaweed and driftwood littered the muddy foreshore.

meanings.definition: Easy to manage, teach, or control; obedient.
examples: The docile pony was perfect for young riders.
Years of routine had made the workers docile and compliant.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characteristic of a city or town.
examples: Urban traffic grows heavier every year.
She traded rural quiet for the bustle of urban life.

meanings.definition: To bring about a settlement by acting as a go-between for opposing parties.
examples: A neutral envoy was asked to mediate the dispute.
She mediated between the two feuding departments.

meanings.definition: A place where butter, cheese, and other dairy products are made or sold.
examples: Fresh butter was churned daily at the village creamery.
The creamery sold rich cream and farmhouse cheese.

meanings.definition: The act of moving or going downward.
examples: The plane began its slow descent toward the runway.
Their descent down the steep trail took most of the afternoon.

meanings.definition: One's family origin or ancestry.
examples: She is of Irish descent on her mother's side.

meanings.definition: A formal agreement or engagement to be married.
examples: Their betrothal was announced at the family banquet.
The betrothal was arranged when they were still children.

meanings.definition: Relating to the growth and life processes of plants.
examples: Warm rain spurred vigorous vegetative growth in the fields.
The plant entered its vegetative stage before flowering.

meanings.definition: A leader or official who defends the rights and interests of ordinary people.
examples: The fiery tribune championed the cause of the poor.
As tribune, he vowed to protect the common citizens.

meanings.definition: To declare someone not guilty and free them from a charge.
examples: The jury voted to acquit him of all charges.
She was acquitted after the evidence collapsed.

meanings.definition: To conduct oneself in a particular way, especially well.
examples: The young soldier acquitted himself bravely in battle.

meanings.definition: Great pleasure or delight.
examples: The band played a final encore for the crowd's delectation.
She read the letters aloud for the family's delectation.

meanings.definition: Not working properly or containing flaws; imperfect.
examples: The accident was caused by faulty brakes.
His argument rested on faulty assumptions.

meanings.definition: Relating to land, farming, or rural matters.
examples: The country's agrarian economy depended on its harvests.
They championed agrarian reform to redistribute farmland.

meanings.definition: A rigid bar that pivots on a support to lift or move a load, or anything used to exert power.
examples: He pulled the lever to release the trapdoor.
Public opinion became a powerful lever for change.

meanings.definition: To move or pry something using a lever.
examples: They levered the boulder aside with a stout iron bar.

meanings.definition: A short dagger with a slender, tapering blade.
examples: The assassin concealed a stiletto in his sleeve.
A thin stiletto lay beside the fallen guard.

meanings.definition: A thin, high, tapering heel on a shoe.
examples: She crossed the marble floor in click-clacking stilettos.

meanings.definition: To give a false impression of; to misrepresent.
examples: Her cheerful tone belied her true anxiety.
His calm face belied the panic he felt inside.

meanings.definition: To show something to be false or untrue.
examples: The new evidence belied the official account of events.

meanings.definition: Overly eager to argue or to settle disputes through lawsuits; quarrelsome.
examples: The litigious neighbor sued over every minor grievance.
Their litigious nature kept the lawyers permanently busy.

meanings.definition: A person who asks for something humbly and earnestly.
examples: The supplicant knelt before the king to beg for mercy.
Crowds of supplicants gathered at the shrine seeking healing.

meanings.definition: To improve someone morally, intellectually, or spiritually.
examples: The lecture was meant to edify as much as to entertain.
She read uplifting books hoping to edify her children.

meanings.definition: Specialized or technical language that is hard for outsiders to understand.
examples: The manual was full of confusing technical jargon.
He cut through the legal jargon to explain the contract plainly.

meanings.definition: The process of changing from one state, place, or condition to another.
examples: The transition from school to work can be difficult.
A smooth transition of power followed the election.

meanings.definition: To change or pass from one state or stage to another.
examples: The company transitioned to fully remote work last year.

meanings.definition: Refusing or failing to obey rules or authority.
examples: The disobedient pupil ignored every warning.
A disobedient dog can be a danger near traffic.

meanings.definition: The condition of being grossly overweight or excessively fat.
examples: Doctors warned that childhood obesity was on the rise.
Poor diet and inactivity contributed to his obesity.

meanings.definition: Lacking the basic necessities of life; very poor.
examples: The charity distributes food to needy families.
She volunteered to help needy children in the city.

meanings.definition: Wanting an excessive amount of attention or affection.
examples: He became clingy and needy after the breakup.

meanings.definition: Not controlled or restrained; unchecked.
examples: The crowd reacted with unbridled enthusiasm.
His unbridled ambition led him to take reckless risks.

meanings.definition: A detailed analysis and assessment of something, especially a work or idea.
examples: The professor offered a sharp critique of the essay.
Her critique of the policy highlighted several flaws.

meanings.definition: To evaluate something in a detailed, critical way.
examples: The students gathered to critique each other's paintings.

meanings.definition: Rich, sweet, and highly pleasing to taste or smell.
examples: The luscious peaches were so ripe that juice dripped down our hands.
She served a luscious chocolate cake for dessert.

meanings.definition: Sensually appealing or richly attractive to the senses.
examples: The garden was filled with luscious green foliage after the rains.

meanings.definition: An oven or furnace used to bake, burn, harden, or dry materials such as clay, bricks, or pottery.
examples: The potter fired the bowls in a kiln overnight.
Bricks are hardened in a kiln at very high temperatures.

meanings.definition: Originating in or characteristic of a distant foreign country.
examples: The market sold exotic fruits we had never seen before.
She decorated her home with exotic plants from the tropics.

meanings.definition: Strikingly unusual, colorful, or excitingly different.
examples: He told exotic tales of his travels across remote islands.

meanings.definition: A flat geometric figure with five straight sides and five angles.
examples: The students measured each angle of the pentagon in geometry class.
A regular pentagon has five equal sides.

meanings.definition: Relating to a whole class, genus, or group rather than to a specific member.
examples: "Fruit" is a generic term that includes apples, oranges, and grapes.
The report used generic language that applied to every department.

meanings.definition: Not sold or protected under a brand name.
examples: The pharmacist offered a cheaper generic version of the medicine.

meanings.definition: Stubbornly resistant to control, authority, or discipline.
examples: The refractory student ignored every instruction the teacher gave.
Their refractory mule refused to move no matter how they coaxed it.

meanings.definition: Difficult to fuse, melt, or treat, especially at high temperatures.
examples: The furnace was lined with refractory bricks that withstand intense heat.

meanings.definition: Achieving maximum results with little wasted effort, time, or resources.
examples: The new engine is far more efficient and uses less fuel.
She is an efficient worker who finishes tasks quickly and accurately.

meanings.definition: First in rank, importance, or quality.
examples: The city's premier restaurant is booked months in advance.
He is the premier expert in his field of research.

meanings.definition: The head of government or chief minister of a country or state.
examples: The premier addressed the nation about the new economic plan.

meanings.definition: To scold or criticize someone sharply for a fault or wrongdoing.
examples: The coach upbraided the players for their careless mistakes.
She upbraided him for arriving late again.

meanings.definition: Showing compassion, pity, or forgiveness toward others.
examples: The merciful judge gave the first-time offender a lighter sentence.
She was merciful and forgave the friend who had wronged her.

meanings.definition: A social gathering at which guests wear masks and costumes to disguise themselves.
examples: They wore elaborate gowns and feathered masks to the masquerade.
The masquerade ball was the highlight of the winter season.

meanings.definition: A false outward show meant to conceal one's true nature or intentions.
examples: His friendly manner was just a masquerade to hide his ambition.

meanings.definition: Childishly irritable, sulky, or bad-tempered over small things.
examples: The petulant child stamped his feet when told to leave the park.
She gave a petulant reply when her plans were questioned.

meanings.definition: Incapable of being wrong or making mistakes in judgment.
examples: No expert is infallible, so even the best can err.
She treated her teacher's opinions as if they were infallible.

meanings.definition: Certain to work or never failing in effect.
examples: He claimed to have an infallible method for memorizing vocabulary.

meanings.definition: A flat geometric figure with ten straight sides and ten angles.
examples: The puzzle piece was shaped like a perfect decagon.
A regular decagon has ten equal sides and ten equal angles.

meanings.definition: A showy display intended to impress others or attract admiration.
examples: The mansion was decorated with vulgar ostentation to flaunt their wealth.
He spent without any ostentation, despite his fortune.

meanings.definition: To bet money or something of value on an uncertain outcome.
examples: He liked to gamble on horse races every weekend.
They gambled their savings on a risky new business.

meanings.definition: To take a risky action in the hope of a favorable result.
examples: She gambled on changing careers, and it paid off.

meanings.definition: To describe or present something as smaller or less important than it really is.
examples: The report understated the true cost of the project.
He tends to understate his achievements out of modesty.

meanings.definition: A unit of length equal to six feet, used chiefly for measuring the depth of water.
examples: The anchor sank several fathoms before reaching the seabed.
The wreck lies many fathoms beneath the surface.

meanings.definition: To understand something deeply after careful thought.
examples: I could not fathom why she had left so suddenly.

meanings.definition: To formally withdraw or renounce a belief or statement one previously held.
examples: Under pressure, the scientist refused to recant his theory.
She publicly recanted her earlier accusations.

meanings.definition: Behaving irrationally or wildly, as if having lost one's mind.
examples: He drove with demented speed through the narrow streets.
The demented look in his eyes frightened the children.

meanings.definition: Excessively unwilling to spend money or use resources; stingy.
examples: Their parsimonious landlord refused to fix the broken heater.
He was so parsimonious that he reused every scrap of paper.

meanings.definition: To transfer a fluid, especially blood, from one body or vessel into another.
examples: Doctors transfused blood into the injured patient.
The nurse carefully transfused the solution into the second container.

meanings.definition: To instill or impart a quality or feeling into someone.
examples: The new coach transfused fresh energy into the team.

meanings.definition: Happening or done at an especially favorable or suitable time.
examples: She made her request at an opportune moment, when the boss was in a good mood.
The rain came at an opportune time for the thirsty crops.

meanings.definition: Of little value or importance; petty and trivial.
examples: He wasted hours arguing over picayune details.
The fine was a picayune sum compared to their wealth.

meanings.definition: A unit of length equal to ten meters.
examples: The garden plot measured one decameter along each side.
They marked the track in decameters for the survey.

meanings.definition: The act of going or putting something completely under the surface of a liquid.
examples: The submersion of the village followed the building of the dam.
Prolonged submersion in cold water is dangerous.

meanings.definition: The body of voters or residents in a district represented by an elected official.
examples: The senator returned home to meet with her constituency.
A large part of his constituency supported the new policy.

meanings.definition: A group of supporters or people with a shared interest.
examples: The proposal appealed to the company's younger constituency.

meanings.definition: A situation requiring a difficult choice between two undesirable or conflicting options.
examples: She faced the dilemma of telling the truth or protecting her friend.
The mayor's dilemma was whether to raise taxes or cut services.

meanings.definition: A comparison between two things based on a resemblance used to explain or reason.
examples: The teacher drew an analogy between the heart and a pump.
By analogy, learning a language is like building a house brick by brick.

meanings.definition: Conformity to accepted standards of behavior, manners, or morality.
examples: She always behaved with great propriety at formal events.
They questioned the propriety of accepting such an expensive gift.

meanings.definition: A unit of mass equal to ten grams.
examples: The recipe called for a decagram of the rare spice.
The sample weighed exactly one decagram on the scale.

meanings.definition: The use of a microscope to examine objects too small to see with the naked eye.
examples: Advances in microscopy allowed scientists to study individual cells.
She specialized in microscopy of tissue samples.

meanings.definition: A shimmering, rainbow-like play of colors that shifts as the angle of view changes.
examples: The iridescence of the soap bubble delighted the children.
A peacock's feathers show a brilliant iridescence in sunlight.

meanings.definition: A calm, balanced, and self-assured manner.
examples: She answered the difficult questions with remarkable poise.
Despite the chaos, he kept his poise throughout the crisis.

meanings.definition: To hold or be held in a balanced, ready position.
examples: The dancer poised on the tips of her toes.

meanings.definition: To seize and take legal possession of property, especially until a debt is paid or a dispute is settled.
examples: The court ordered officials to sequestrate the company's assets.
Authorities sequestrated the land during the legal dispute.

meanings.definition: To declare or set apart as sacred for a religious purpose.
examples: The bishop consecrated the new church on Sunday.
The ground was consecrated as a burial site.

meanings.definition: To dedicate something wholly to a particular purpose.
examples: She consecrated her life to helping the poor.

meanings.definition: The act of bringing something to an end or the point at which it ends.
examples: The termination of the contract surprised both parties.
Repeated lateness led to the termination of his employment.

meanings.definition: Laid down or established beforehand as a rule or model to be followed.
examples: The soldiers followed the prescript drill without deviation.
Each ceremony was conducted in the prescript manner.

meanings.definition: The belief in or worship of more than one god.
examples: Ancient Greek religion was a form of polytheism with many gods.
Polytheism contrasts sharply with monotheistic faiths.

meanings.definition: Feeling or showing deep respect and humble admiration.
examples: The visitors fell into a reverent silence inside the cathedral.
He spoke of his late teacher in a reverent tone.

meanings.definition: Highest in position, rank, or importance.
examples: Safety was uppermost in the pilot's mind during the storm.
The uppermost branches of the tree swayed in the wind.

meanings.definition: A small group of people secretly united to plot or scheme for a private purpose.
examples: A cabal of advisers plotted to remove the king.
Rumors spread of a cabal controlling the company's decisions.

meanings.definition: Belonging to a people or nation that is not Jewish.
examples: The festival welcomed both Jewish and gentile neighbors.
He grew up in a largely gentile community.

meanings.definition: A person who is not Jewish.
examples: The synagogue often hosted gentiles curious about its traditions.

meanings.definition: The state of being calm and in control of one's emotions.
examples: She kept her composure even as the audience grew restless.
He lost his composure and shouted at the referee.

meanings.definition: The reinforcement or prolonging of sound by sympathetic vibration.
examples: The hall's resonance gave the orchestra a rich, full sound.
The deep resonance of the drum filled the room.

meanings.definition: A quality that evokes a strong emotional or personal response.
examples: The poem had a special resonance for those who had lost loved ones.

meanings.definition: A principle or set of beliefs laid down by an authority as unquestionably true.
examples: He rejected the church's dogma about the afterlife.
Scientific inquiry should challenge accepted dogma.

meanings.definition: Tending to use up or gradually destroy something.
examples: The factory's consumptive use of water drained the local river.
Such consumptive habits steadily depleted their savings.

meanings.definition: Affected by or relating to tuberculosis (a disease once called consumption).
examples: The pale, consumptive patient coughed throughout the night.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characteristic of an island.
examples: The insular climate was mild and damp year-round.
Insular communities developed their own distinct customs.

meanings.definition: Narrow-minded and uninterested in cultures or ideas outside one's own.
examples: Their insular attitude made them suspicious of all outsiders.

meanings.definition: The medieval social system in which lords granted land to vassals in exchange for service and loyalty.
examples: Under feudalism, peasants worked the land owned by powerful lords.
Feudalism shaped European society for centuries.

meanings.definition: Bold or reckless daring, often to the point of audacity.
examples: He had the hardihood to question the general's orders.
Her hardihood in the face of danger amazed everyone.

meanings.definition: Failure or refusal to obey rules, orders, or authority.
examples: His repeated disobedience got him suspended from school.
Acts of civil disobedience helped change unjust laws.

meanings.definition: Giving or showing favor or priority to a particular person or group.
examples: Loyal customers received preferential discounts.
Critics accused the official of preferential treatment toward friends.

meanings.definition: To show or reveal a quality or feeling clearly.
examples: Her speech evinced a deep love for her homeland.
The students evinced little interest in the dull lecture.

meanings.definition: Something that sticks out, or the act of sticking out from a surface.
examples: A small protrusion on the rock made a useful handhold.
The protrusion of the nail caused him to trip.

meanings.definition: A person who makes a claim or demand, especially as a legal right.
examples: The claimant filed for compensation after the accident.
Several claimants came forward to inherit the estate.

meanings.definition: Able to bend easily without breaking.
examples: The flexible wire could be twisted into any shape.
Gymnasts have remarkably flexible bodies.

meanings.definition: Able to adapt easily to change or new circumstances.
examples: Our flexible schedule lets us work from home when needed.

meanings.definition: A defensive wall or embankment built to protect a fort or city against attack.
examples: Soldiers stood guard along the castle's ramparts.
The ancient city was encircled by massive ramparts.

meanings.definition: The Pope, the head of the Roman Catholic Church; a chief bishop.
examples: Crowds gathered to hear the pontiff speak in the square.
The pontiff blessed the pilgrims gathered below.

meanings.definition: Official permission for a ship, aircraft, or person to proceed.
examples: The pilot waited for clearance before taking off.
The vessel received clearance to leave the harbor.

meanings.definition: The amount of clear space or distance between two objects.
examples: There was barely enough clearance for the truck under the bridge.

meanings.definition: A ruler or lord who holds power and authority over others.
examples: The villagers paid tribute to their overlord each harvest.
The region was ruled by a ruthless overlord.

meanings.definition: The act of making a deliberate choice or decision by one's own free will.
examples: She left the company of her own volition, not because she was fired.
Acting on his own volition, he donated all his savings to charity.

meanings.definition: A person who copies or mimics the behavior, style, or work of another.
examples: The young singer was a skilled imitator of famous performers.
Many imitators tried to copy the artist's unique style.

meanings.definition: Relating to the mind, soul, or mental processes.
examples: The trauma left deep psychic scars that took years to heal.
She studied the psychic effects of long isolation.

meanings.definition: Relating to supposed supernatural mental powers beyond ordinary senses.
examples: He claimed to have psychic abilities that let him read minds.

meanings.definition: Inclined to find fault and judge others severely or harshly.
examples: Her censorious remarks made everyone feel criticized.
The censorious critic rarely had a kind word for any film.

meanings.definition: Showing great attention to detail and correct behavior or procedure.
examples: He was punctilious about answering every email the same day.
The host was punctilious in observing every point of etiquette.

meanings.definition: A citizen or respectable inhabitant of a town, especially in former times.
examples: The wealthy burghers of the medieval town funded the cathedral.
As a burgher, he had the right to vote in town affairs.

meanings.definition: Existing or operating in the mind below the level of conscious awareness.
examples: His subconscious fear of failure shaped many of his decisions.
A subconscious habit made her tap her pen during the test.

meanings.definition: A political leader who wins support by appealing to popular emotions and prejudices rather than reason.
examples: The demagogue stirred up the crowd with angry, divisive speeches.
Voters eventually saw through the demagogue's empty promises.

meanings.definition: Having the character of a beginner or newcomer.
examples: His neophyte enthusiasm made up for his lack of experience.
The neophyte programmer eagerly learned from her mentors.

meanings.definition: A person who is new to an activity, belief, or skill.
examples: As a neophyte, she asked many questions during training.

meanings.definition: Cleverness and skill in inventing, designing, or solving problems.
examples: The engineers showed great ingenuity in fixing the bridge cheaply.
With remarkable ingenuity, she built a radio from spare parts.

meanings.definition: Distant and unsympathetic, not wanting to associate with others.
examples: She stayed aloof from her classmates, rarely joining their conversations.
His aloof manner made him seem cold to people who didn't know him.

meanings.definition: At a distance, apart from others.
examples: He stood aloof while the rest of the team celebrated.

meanings.definition: The state of being greater in number, power, or importance than others.
examples: The predominance of small farms shaped the region's economy.
Critics noted the predominance of male voices in the debate.

meanings.definition: To try to persuade someone to do something, especially something wrong or unwise, by offering them a reward or pleasure.
examples: The smell of fresh bread tempted her to break her diet.
Don't let easy money tempt you into a dishonest deal.

meanings.definition: To make someone want to do or have something appealing.
examples: The sunny weather tempted us to go for a long walk.

meanings.definition: To signal to someone with a nod or gesture.
examples: She becked him toward the open doorway.
The host becked the waiter with a quick wave of his hand.

meanings.definition: To produce or be filled with a deep, full, echoing sound.
examples: The bell's tone resonated through the empty hall.
His voice resonated in the stone chamber.

meanings.definition: To affect or appeal to someone in a personal or meaningful way.
examples: The speaker's message resonated with the young audience.

meanings.definition: To put or insert something into something else.
examples: The surgeon carefully intromitted the probe into the vein.
A thin tube was intromitted to drain the fluid.

meanings.definition: A figure of speech that compares one thing to another using the words 'like' or 'as'.
examples: "As brave as a lion" is a common simile.
The poet used a vivid simile to describe the storm.

meanings.definition: A person who watches an event, game, or show.
examples: Thousands of spectators filled the stadium for the final.
A few curious spectators gathered to watch the street performer.

meanings.definition: A feeling of doubt or unease about whether one is doing the right thing.
examples: She had no qualms about telling him the truth.
He felt a sudden qualm before signing the contract.

meanings.definition: A brief, sudden feeling of sickness or nausea.
examples: A qualm passed over him as the boat rocked in the waves.

meanings.definition: A page size produced by folding a sheet of paper twice to form four leaves, or a book of that size.
examples: The rare quarto sold at auction for a record price.
Early plays were often printed in quarto editions.

meanings.definition: The wide part of a river where it meets the sea and fresh water mixes with salt water.
examples: Many birds nest along the muddy banks of the estuary.
The river widens into a broad estuary before reaching the ocean.

meanings.definition: Able to be conducted or transmitted, as heat or electricity.
examples: Copper is highly conductible and ideal for wiring.
Heat is readily conductible through metal.

meanings.definition: Happening or produced once every three months.
examples: The company issues a quarterly report to its shareholders.
She pays her insurance in quarterly installments.

meanings.definition: Once every three months.
examples: Interest is paid quarterly into the account.

meanings.definition: Having or resembling a human form.
examples: The ancient statue had an anthropomorphous shape with arms and legs.
Many myths describe anthropomorphous gods who look like people.

meanings.definition: A group of vehicles or ships traveling together, often with an armed escort for protection.
examples: A convoy of trucks carried supplies to the flooded town.
The merchant ships crossed the ocean in a guarded convoy.

meanings.definition: Something that makes it harder to do something or that gets in the way.
examples: The heavy traffic was a hindrance to our journey.
Lack of funding proved a serious hindrance to the project.

meanings.definition: To defeat or gain control over someone by greater strength or force.
examples: The guards managed to overpower the intruder.
A larger army quickly overpowered the defenders.

meanings.definition: To be so strong or intense as to overwhelm the senses.
examples: The smell of the perfume overpowered everyone in the room.

meanings.definition: To search into or examine something thoroughly.
examples: Investigators probed the cause of the accident.
She probed the matter with careful, pointed questions.

meanings.definition: A thorough investigation into something.
examples: The committee launched a probe into the missing funds.

meanings.definition: To prick or sting slightly with many small, sharp points.
examples: The wool sweater prickled against her skin.
Tears prickled at the corners of his eyes.

meanings.definition: A small, sharp point on a plant or animal.
examples: The cactus was covered in tiny prickles.

meanings.definition: A loyal and obedient follower who carries out the orders of a powerful person, often for wrongdoing.
examples: The crime boss sent his henchmen to collect the debt.
One of the villain's henchmen guarded the door.

meanings.definition: Frequently changing; not staying the same or remaining faithful.
examples: His inconstant moods made him hard to please.
She accused him of being inconstant in his affections.

meanings.definition: Advancement or promotion to a higher position or office.
examples: He sought preferment within the church.
Years of loyal service finally earned her preferment.

meanings.definition: Far from where most people go; remote or hard to reach.
examples: They found a charming inn in an out-of-the-way village.
The shop was tucked in an out-of-the-way corner of town.

meanings.definition: The study of the form and structure of organisms.
examples: Plant morphology helps botanists classify different species.
Her research focused on the morphology of insect wings.

meanings.definition: In language, the study of the structure and forms of words.
examples: Morphology explains how prefixes and suffixes change a word's meaning.

meanings.definition: An act that grossly violates morality, decency, or law and provokes anger.
examples: The attack on the hospital was condemned as an outrage.
Destroying the ancient monument was a cultural outrage.

meanings.definition: A strong feeling of anger or shock.
examples: The decision sparked public outrage across the country.

meanings.definition: Bred from pure or superior stock, especially of horses.
examples: She rode a sleek thoroughbred mare in the race.
The stable specializes in thoroughbred horses.

meanings.definition: An animal, especially a horse, of pure breed.
examples: The thoroughbred galloped easily ahead of the field.

meanings.definition: To exist at an earlier time or before something else.
examples: Some philosophers believe the soul preexists the body.
These rock formations preexist all human history.

meanings.definition: A state of extreme misery, unhappiness, or distress.
examples: The poem describes the wretchedness of the poor.
Years of war left the people in utter wretchedness.

meanings.definition: Control or rule over others through the exercise of power or authority.
examples: The empire sought domination over the entire region.
They resisted foreign domination for centuries.

meanings.definition: Excessively critical; finding fault with even small things.
examples: Her hypercritical boss complained about every tiny mistake.
He was so hypercritical that nothing ever satisfied him.

meanings.definition: The range of subjects, area, or extent that something deals with or affects.
examples: The investigation widened in scope as new facts emerged.
That question is beyond the scope of this report.

meanings.definition: The opportunity or freedom to act or develop.
examples: The new role gave her scope to use her creativity.

meanings.definition: The state or quality of being triple or threefold.
examples: The design's triplicity echoed the three founding principles.
Astrologers grouped the signs by their triplicity.

meanings.definition: Careful and constant watchfulness for possible danger or difficulties.
examples: Constant vigilance is needed to keep the borders secure.
The guard's vigilance prevented the theft.

meanings.definition: To be in charge of and direct the running of a task, project, or operation.
examples: She was hired to superintend the building of the bridge.
He superintended the daily operations of the factory.

meanings.definition: The act of forcing or being forced to do something.
examples: He paid the fine under compulsion, not by choice.
No one should marry under compulsion.

meanings.definition: A strong, often irresistible urge to do something.
examples: She felt a sudden compulsion to check the locks again.

meanings.definition: The ability to recover quickly from difficulties or to spring back into shape.
examples: The community showed great resilience after the flood.
The rubber band's resilience let it snap back instantly.

meanings.definition: A person who forms their own opinions about religion rather than accepting established authority or doctrine.
examples: As a freethinker, she questioned the teachings she grew up with.
The philosopher was branded a freethinker by the church.

meanings.definition: To set up or bring back something again after it has been lost or interrupted.
examples: They worked to reestablish trade between the two nations.
After the storm, crews reestablished power to the area.

meanings.definition: Coming together or tending toward a single point.
examples: The convergent roads met at the town square.
Their convergent interests made them natural partners.

meanings.definition: A statement that seems to contradict itself or common belief yet may be true.
examples: It is a paradox that the more you give, the more you have.
The play explores the paradox of freedom within strict rules.

meanings.definition: Electric current produced by chemical action, as in a battery.
examples: Early experiments in galvanism made a frog's leg twitch.
The device was powered by galvanism from a simple cell.

meanings.definition: An unlucky accident or piece of bad luck.
examples: The hikers' misadventure left them lost overnight.
His tale was full of comic misadventures.

meanings.definition: To fight on horseback with lances, as knights did in medieval contests.
examples: The knights jousted before the cheering crowd.
Two riders jousted at the festival in full armor.

meanings.definition: To compete or argue with someone in a spirited way.
examples: The two candidates jousted over the issue during the debate.

meanings.definition: A tool with a long curved blade on a long handle, used for cutting grass or grain by hand.
examples: The farmer cut the wheat with a scythe.
An old scythe hung on the barn wall.

meanings.definition: The current fashion or popular style at a particular time.
examples: Bright colors were in vogue that summer.
Tight jeans came back into vogue last year.

meanings.definition: Using force or threats to make someone do something.
examples: The regime relied on coercive measures to silence critics.
He used coercive tactics to win the agreement.

meanings.definition: A prominent object or feature that helps people find their way or recognize a place.
examples: The old clock tower is a well-known landmark in the city.
We used the church spire as a landmark to find our way back.

meanings.definition: An event or achievement marking an important stage or turning point.
examples: The treaty was a landmark in the history of the two nations.

meanings.definition: To make amends or pay the penalty for a wrong or sin.
examples: He spent years trying to expiate his guilt.
She gave to charity to expiate her past mistakes.

meanings.definition: A stringed musical instrument slightly larger than a violin and lower in pitch.
examples: She plays the viola in the school orchestra.
The viola carried the rich middle harmony of the quartet.

meanings.definition: A general rule, principle, or standard by which something is judged.
examples: Honesty is a basic canon of good journalism.
The essay broke every canon of polite debate.

meanings.definition: The body of works generally accepted as genuine or important.
examples: The play is now part of the literary canon.
